news_summary: Today's technology news highlights groundbreaking advancements in understanding fundamental life processes, revolutionary shifts in mathematics, innovative subsonic vehicle development, and pioneering data centers utilizing human brain cells.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées révolutionnaires dans la compréhension des processus fondamentaux de la vie, des changements révolutionnaires en mathématiques, le développement innovant de véhicules subsoniques et des centres de données pionniers utilisant des cellules du cerveau humain.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan avances revolucionarios en la comprensión de los procesos fundamentales de la vida, cambios revolucionarios en las matemáticas, el desarrollo innovador de vehículos subsónicos y centros de datos pioneros que utilizan células del cerebro humano.

articles.title: ‘Virtual cell’ captures most-basic process of life: bacterial division
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 10 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00786-4Researchers simulated nearly every molecule in a bacterial cell — and then watched the cell grow and reproduce.
articles.content:
A computer generated illustration of a simulated cell in the early stages of division. Credit: Zane Thornburg

For the first time, researchers have simulated nearly every chemical reaction in a living bacterial cell. The simulation models the virtual cell copying its DNA and dividing in two.

The simulation could help researchers understand how interacting mix of proteins, nucleic acids, fats and other molecules within the wall of a cell gives rise to actual life, says Zane Thornburg, a computational biophysicist at the University of Illinois in Urbana-Champaign who co-led the 9 March study in Cell1.

Virtual life

To model bacterial life, Thornburg turned to one of its simplest examples: a bacterial cell with a ‘minimal’ genome. The organism, named JCVI-Syn3a, was created by whittling the genome of the parasite Mycoplasma mycoides down to just 493 genes, dispensing with more than 400 non-essential genes2.

Thornburg created a three-dimensional simulation that attempted to account for the cell’s DNA, proteins, ribosomes and other molecules of life, as they change over time. Specific molecules, such as a DNA-copying enzyme, obeyed rules based on real-world measurements, and reactions occurred when interacting partners became close in physical space.

Some details were fudged. For example, the functions of several dozen JCVI-Syn3a genes still aren’t known, so Thornburg’s team modelled them as inert spheres. Also, in real cells, multiple ribosomes can make protein using the same mRNA transcript, but Thornburg’s model allowed just one ribosome per transcript.

The team’s goal was to simulate JCVI-Syn3a over the time it takes to copy its DNA and divide into two, known as the cell cycle. Some early attempts failed, Thornburg says, because the genome kept falling apart faster than it could be made, or spewing out of the cell membrane.

After making tweaks to address such issues, they left the model to run over US Thanksgiving holiday in November. “We came back, and suddenly a whole cell cycle had run,” Thornburg says. “All of a sudden, it was just this huge leap.”

The computer simulation recapitulated many details of actual life, including how the shape of the cell swells and elongates as it divides into two. The 105 minutes the virtual cell took to divide was “scarily close” to the time the cell takes to reproduce in real life, Thornburg says. Simulating those 105 minutes, however, took 6 days on a supercomputer, underscoring how computationally intensive such cell models are to run.

Bernhard Palsson, a bioengineer at the University of California, San Diego, says the simulation is an important achievement because of the shear breadth of cellular activity it captures. “Getting all these processes to behave coherently during the cell cycle is a major challenge.”

Alternative models
articles.reasoning: The article reports a major scientific breakthrough in simulating nearly every chemical reaction in a living bacterial cell, enabling a detailed understanding of the fundamental process of life and cell division. This achievement has broad significance for biology and medicine, providing a powerful tool for research with potential wide-ranging benefits. The article focuses on this single, substantial advancement and provides detailed context about the simulation and its implications.
articles.personality_title: Scientists simulate a full bacterial cell dividing for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long wanted to understand exactly how life works inside cells. Bacterial cells are the simplest living things, making them a good place to start. But until now, no one had been able to simulate nearly every chemical reaction happening inside a whole cell as it grows and divides.

**What happened** – A team led by Zane Thornburg at the University of Illinois created a virtual model of a simple bacterial cell called JCVI-Syn3a. This cell has only 493 genes, much fewer than normal bacteria. Using a supercomputer, the team simulated how the cell copies its DNA and splits into two new cells. The simulation took six days to run and showed the cell dividing in about 105 minutes, very close to the real-life time.

**Impact** – This is the first time a computer has simulated almost every molecule and chemical reaction inside a living bacterial cell during division. The model shows how proteins, DNA, and other molecules work together to make life happen. This detailed simulation helps scientists study life’s most basic processes in a way never done before.

**What's next step** – Researchers hope to improve the model by adding more details and fixing parts that are still unknown. They also want to use the simulation to understand how cells respond to medicines or changes in their environment. This could help design better drugs or new treatments in the future.

**One-sentence takeaway** – For the first time, scientists have used a computer to recreate nearly every step of a bacterial cell’s life cycle, opening new doors to studying life at its smallest level.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques simulent pour la première fois la division complète d'une cellule bactérienne
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent depuis longtemps à comprendre exactement comment fonctionne la vie à l'intérieur des cellules. Les cellules bactériennes sont les formes de vie les plus simples, ce qui en fait un bon point de départ. Mais jusqu'à présent, personne n'avait réussi à simuler presque toutes les réactions chimiques dans une cellule entière pendant sa croissance et sa division.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une équipe dirigée par Zane Thornburg à l'Université de l'Illinois a créé un modèle virtuel d'une cellule bactérienne simple appelée JCVI-Syn3a. Cette cellule possède seulement 493 gènes, bien moins que les bactéries normales. Grâce à un superordinateur, l'équipe a simulé comment la cellule copie son ADN et se divise en deux nouvelles cellules. La simulation a duré six jours et a montré une division en environ 105 minutes, très proche du temps réel.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois qu'un ordinateur simule presque toutes les molécules et réactions chimiques à l'intérieur d'une cellule bactérienne vivante pendant sa division. Le modèle montre comment les protéines, l'ADN et d'autres molécules travaillent ensemble pour créer la vie. Cette simulation détaillée aide les scientifiques à étudier les processus fondamentaux de la vie comme jamais auparavant.

**La prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs espèrent améliorer le modèle en ajoutant plus de détails et en corrigeant les parties encore inconnues. Ils veulent aussi utiliser la simulation pour comprendre comment les cellules réagissent aux médicaments ou aux changements de l'environnement. Cela pourrait aider à concevoir de meilleurs médicaments ou de nouveaux traitements.

**Une phrase pour retenir** – Pour la première fois, des scientifiques ont recréé presque toutes les étapes du cycle de vie d'une cellule bactérienne grâce à un ordinateur, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour étudier la vie à son plus petit niveau.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos simulan por primera vez la división completa de una célula bacteriana
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han querido durante mucho tiempo entender exactamente cómo funciona la vida dentro de las células. Las células bacterianas son las formas de vida más simples, por lo que son un buen punto de partida. Pero hasta ahora, nadie había podido simular casi todas las reacciones químicas dentro de una célula completa mientras crece y se divide.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo liderado por Zane Thornburg en la Universidad de Illinois creó un modelo virtual de una célula bacteriana simple llamada JCVI-Syn3a. Esta célula tiene solo 493 genes, mucho menos que las bacterias normales. Usando una supercomputadora, el equipo simuló cómo la célula copia su ADN y se divide en dos nuevas células. La simulación tomó seis días y mostró que la división dura alrededor de 105 minutos, muy cerca del tiempo real.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que una computadora simula casi todas las moléculas y reacciones químicas dentro de una célula bacteriana viva durante la división. El modelo muestra cómo las proteínas, el ADN y otras moléculas trabajan juntas para crear la vida. Esta simulación detallada ayuda a los científicos a estudiar los procesos más básicos de la vida de una forma nunca antes vista.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores esperan mejorar el modelo añadiendo más detalles y corrigiendo las partes que aún se desconocen. También quieren usar la simulación para entender cómo responden las células a medicamentos o cambios en su ambiente. Esto podría ayudar a diseñar mejores medicamentos o nuevos tratamientos.

**Una frase clave** – Por primera vez, los científicos recrearon con una computadora casi todos los pasos del ciclo de vida de una célula bacteriana, abriendo nuevas puertas para estudiar la vida a su nivel más pequeño.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a translucent, glowing bacterial cell gently elongating and beginning to split into two, with intricate swirling strands of DNA and clusters of tiny, abstract molecular shapes inside symbolizing proteins and ribosomes, all rendered in soft natural colors against a simple, dark background to highlight the delicate complexity of life’s fundamental process.

articles.title: Mathematics is undergoing the biggest change in its history
articles.summary: The speed at which artificial intelligence is gaining in mathematical ability has taken many by surprise. It is rewriting what it means to be a mathematician
articles.content:
The speed at which artificial intelligence is gaining in mathematical ability has taken many by surprise. It is rewriting what it means to be a mathematician

Are the days of handwritten mathematics coming to an end? Laborant / Alamy

In March 2025, mathematician Daniel Litt made a bet. Despite the march of progress of artificial intelligence in many fields, he believed his subject was safe, wagering with a colleague that there was only a 25 per cent chance an AI could write a mathematical paper at the level of the best human mathematicians by 2030. Only a year later, he thinks he was wrong. “I now expect to lose this bet,” he declared on his blog.

Mathematicians have been taken aback by the speed of improvements in AI’s ability to solve problems and produce proofs. “A couple of years ago, they were basically useless for even solving high school math problems, and now they can sometimes solve problems that really appear in the research life of a mathematician,” says Litt, who is at the University of Toronto.

This progress is faster than many had predicted, with mathematicians warning that their profession is undergoing one of the fastest evolutions the field has ever seen. “We are running out of places to hide,” wrote Jeremy Avigad at Carnegie Mellon University in Pennsylvania in a recent essay. “We have to face up to the fact that AI will soon be able to prove theorems better than we can.”

It isn’t a single event that is causing reactions like this, but the steady mathematical progress AIs are demonstrating. Last year, companies like OpenAI and Google DeepMind achieved gold-medal performances on the International Mathematical Olympiad, an elite competition for high-school students that many experts had previously written off as being beyond the purview of AI tools. In January, mathematicians began using similar tools to solve long-standing problems posed by the Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős.

Now, in two separate developments, AI has begun to take on more complex mathematics, solving real research problems and helping to automatically verify cutting-edge proofs, which traditionally could take a huge amount of work from teams of mathematicians.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

In February, Nikhil Srivastava at the University of California, Berkeley, and his colleagues started the First Proof project in an attempt to find a more realistic benchmark with which to test AI’s mathematical aptitude. The first round of the project consisted of 10 problems that researchers had needed to solve as part of their work, from wildly different mathematical fields.

“They were naturally occurring problems in our day-to-day research,” says Srivastava. “They’re drawn from a typical distribution of difficulty. They weren’t super hard, but they weren’t routine either. There was really a range.”

Proof of progress

After the problems were made public, solutions began to pour in. Researchers at tech companies, including OpenAI and Google DeepMind, were among those who attempted to solve the First Proof problems with their own AI models. OpenAI claims it answered half of the problems correctly, according to “feedback from experts”, while Google DeepMind scored 6 out of 10, according to mathematicians it consulted for each problem.

“Things have changed so fast,” says Thang Luong at Google DeepMind. “For us, now AI has really become a serious collaborator, either to produce serious research work or, in the case of First Proof, it can also actually propose a solution by itself.”

Google’s AI maths tool, called Aletheia, uses a computationally intensive version of its Gemini AI chatbot, paired with a verification algorithm to look for flaws in possible solutions. It can then iteratively produce improvements by itself until it arrives at an answer. Google hasn’t publicly disclosed how many iterations Aletheia needed to solve those problems, which makes it difficult to assess just how good it is, but mathematicians are still impressed.

Not all the problems were unanimously agreed as being solved. With problem 8, for example, which is in a niche area of geometry, only five of the seven experts that Google asked agreed that the proposed solution was correct. Ivan Smith at the University of Cambridge, who wasn’t involved in the Google effort, says the AI does appear to be taking a sensible approach to this problem and shows good progress. “If this was a PhD student coming back with their thoughts, it would be encouraging and would build confidence that the result was actually true,” says Smith.

This highlights a problem with AI-generated proofs – checking them is hard work. It would be easy to get into a situation where AI can generate proofs faster than humans can check them. If a theorem is proved by an AI, but nobody is around to check it, has it been proved? AI can help here, too.

The technology is quickly improving at translating handwritten proofs in natural language, like the problems posed in First Proof, into a format that can be checked by a computer, a process called formalisation.

The AI company Math, Inc. recently took mathematicians by surprise by announcing that its AI tool, called Gauss, had formalised an award-winning proof and verified it was correct. The proof concerned how many spheres can be packed into a space and was the subject of Maryna Viazovska’s 2022 Fields medal, often called the Nobel prize of mathematics.

The effort to formalise Viazovska’s work began with a small group of mathematicians at the end of 2024, working separately from Math. Inc, who hoped to manually translate the problem into computer code. They first looked at Viazovska’s sphere packing-solution in eight dimensions. While they were making steady progress, Math, Inc., which had later provided assistance to the researchers, announced it already had a full proof, and then, a more general version of a result for 24 dimensions.

Bhavik Mehta at Imperial College London and his colleagues had originally sketched out a rough plan of how to formalise the work, as well as come up with important mathematical definitions. Without this, the AI couldn’t have completed its proofs, says Mehta.

“We had made all the pieces, but we hadn’t written the instruction manual that explains how to put them together,” says Chris Birkbeck at the University of East Anglia, UK, also part of the team.

A new style of mathematician

The final proof was around 200,000 lines of code, which constitutes about 10 per cent of all existing formalised mathematics. Although it is likely that this code is about 10 times longer than a human would have produced to do the same task, it is still a huge achievement, says Johan Commelin at Utrecht University in the Netherlands. “This is a big deal. This is Fields medal-winning work, and it’s being auto-formalised.”

Similar efforts should now be possible for a large number of other fields, says Commelin, which could transform how mathematics is practised. “The future that we’re all thinking of is that we’ll have tooling that will automatically formalise new research and mathematical papers, and flag whether there are mistakes or not,” says Commelin. “This will have huge implications for, say, peer review and refereeing work.”

Faced with a future in which an increasing share of mathematics is done by AI, some mathematicians, like Avigad, are raising the alarm about the detrimental effects this might have on our ability to practise and come up with new mathematics.

Using machines to solve the types of problem posed in First Proof may produce concrete proofs, says Anna Marie Bohmann at Vanderbilt University in Tennessee, but we lose the “learning opportunity”, she says. “Struggling to create and formulate new ideas and to solve new problems is one of the main ways in which both students and mathematics professionals consolidate their knowledge.”

Tony Feng, one of the Aletheia team at Google DeepMind, feels similarly and is cautious about using the tool himself. “A lot of times I feel like I should be doing my own homework and going through the process of building my own intuition.”

Even formalising proofs can generate important insights, says Mehta, and he and his colleagues will now need to untangle the 200,000-line AI proof to work out what might be useful for other projects.

But mathematicians are still hopeful there will be a place for them in an increasingly machine-led future. Looking to history, Commelin says that manual calculations were once a large part of being a mathematician, but they are now done automatically. “I think similar things will happen here, where we radically change what we’re doing, but 10 or 20 years from now, we will still recognise what we’re doing as mathematics, in a new style.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant and rapid advancement in AI's mathematical capabilities, demonstrating real-world impact by transforming how mathematical research is conducted. This progress has broad implications for the scientific community and society by potentially accelerating mathematical discovery and verification, which can benefit multiple fields reliant on mathematics. The article provides detailed examples and context about AI solving complex problems, formalizing proofs, and collaborating with mathematicians, making it a substantive and focused report on a major technological breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: AI is changing how mathematicians solve problems and check proofs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) has recently improved its ability to do math much faster than expected. Before, AI could barely solve high school problems, but now it can help with real research questions in math.

**What happened** – In 2025, a mathematician named Daniel Litt bet that AI would not write a top-level math paper by 2030. Just a year later, he changed his mind because AI started solving important math problems and even helped verify complex proofs. Projects by OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and others showed AI solving research problems and formalizing proofs, including one that earned a Fields Medal, a top math award.

**Impact** – This progress is unique because AI is no longer just a tool for simple calculations; it is becoming a serious partner in math research. AI can propose solutions, check proofs, and speed up work that used to take teams of mathematicians months or years. However, some experts worry that relying too much on AI might reduce human learning and creativity.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue to improve AI tools to solve and verify more complex math problems. Mathematicians will also explore how to work alongside AI, using it to check their work while still developing their own understanding. The way math is done may change a lot, but human mathematicians expect to keep playing an important role.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI is quickly becoming a powerful helper in mathematical research, changing how problems are solved and proofs are checked while mathematicians adapt to this new way of working.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'IA transforme la manière dont les mathématiciens résolvent les problèmes et vérifient les preuves
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'intelligence artificielle (IA) a récemment beaucoup progressé en mathématiques, bien plus vite que prévu. Avant, l'IA ne pouvait résoudre que des problèmes simples de niveau lycée, mais maintenant elle aide à résoudre des questions de recherche réelle en mathématiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – En 2025, un mathématicien nommé Daniel Litt a parié que l'IA ne pourrait pas écrire un article mathématique de haut niveau avant 2030. Un an plus tard, il a changé d'avis car l'IA a commencé à résoudre des problèmes importants et à vérifier des preuves complexes. Des projets d'OpenAI, Google DeepMind et d'autres ont montré que l'IA peut résoudre des problèmes de recherche et formaliser des preuves, y compris une preuve récompensée par une médaille Fields, un prix prestigieux en mathématiques.

**Impact** – Cette avancée est unique car l'IA n'est plus seulement un outil pour des calculs simples, elle devient un partenaire sérieux dans la recherche mathématique. L'IA peut proposer des solutions, vérifier des preuves et accélérer un travail qui prenait auparavant des mois ou des années à des équipes de mathématiciens. Cependant, certains experts craignent que trop compter sur l'IA réduise l'apprentissage et la créativité humaines.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à améliorer les outils d'IA pour résoudre et vérifier des problèmes mathématiques plus complexes. Les mathématiciens exploreront comment collaborer avec l'IA, l'utilisant pour vérifier leur travail tout en développant leur propre compréhension. La manière de faire des mathématiques pourrait beaucoup changer, mais les mathématiciens humains pensent qu'ils garderont un rôle important.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L'IA devient rapidement un assistant puissant en recherche mathématique, changeant la façon de résoudre les problèmes et de vérifier les preuves, tandis que les mathématiciens s'adaptent à cette nouvelle méthode de travail.
articles.personality_title_es: La IA cambia cómo los matemáticos resuelven problemas y verifican pruebas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) ha mejorado recientemente su capacidad matemática mucho más rápido de lo esperado. Antes, la IA apenas podía resolver problemas de secundaria, pero ahora ayuda con preguntas reales de investigación en matemáticas.

**Qué pasó** – En 2025, un matemático llamado Daniel Litt apostó que la IA no escribiría un artículo matemático de alto nivel antes de 2030. Solo un año después, cambió de opinión porque la IA comenzó a resolver problemas importantes y a ayudar a verificar pruebas complejas. Proyectos de OpenAI, Google DeepMind y otros mostraron que la IA puede resolver problemas de investigación y formalizar pruebas, incluyendo una que ganó una Medalla Fields, un premio muy importante en matemáticas.

**Impacto** – Este avance es único porque la IA ya no es solo una herramienta para cálculos simples; se está convirtiendo en un colaborador serio en la investigación matemática. La IA puede proponer soluciones, revisar pruebas y acelerar trabajos que antes tomaban meses o años a equipos de matemáticos. Sin embargo, algunos expertos temen que depender demasiado de la IA pueda reducir el aprendizaje y la creatividad humana.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán mejorando las herramientas de IA para resolver y verificar problemas matemáticos más complejos. Los matemáticos explorarán cómo trabajar junto a la IA, usándola para revisar su trabajo mientras desarrollan su propio entendimiento. La forma de hacer matemáticas puede cambiar mucho, pero los matemáticos humanos esperan seguir teniendo un papel importante.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La IA se está convirtiendo rápidamente en una ayuda poderosa en la investigación matemática, cambiando cómo se resuelven problemas y se verifican pruebas mientras los matemáticos se adaptan a esta nueva forma de trabajar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient open mathematics book with flowing handwritten equations transforming into glowing, intricate geometric shapes and abstract proof structures that ascend gently into a radiant, ethereal AI brain made of interconnected circuits and softly glowing nodes, set against a calm, neutral background with natural, soft earth tones and subtle light highlighting the harmonious fusion of human creativity and artificial intelligence in mathematics.

articles.title: About Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools Project
articles.summary: Project Overview NASA’s Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT) project develops technologies and tools for various types of aircraft that fly in different speed regimes, including next-generation vertical take-off and landing and fixed-wing subsonic aircraft. The research advances knowledge, technologies, and concepts that enable major steps to lowering operating costs of the next-generation single-aisle aircraft. […]
articles.content:
An advanced vehicle concept. NASA

Project Overview

NASA’s Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT) project develops technologies and tools for various types of aircraft that fly in different speed regimes, including next-generation vertical take-off and landing and fixed-wing subsonic aircraft.

The research advances knowledge, technologies, and concepts that enable major steps to lowering operating costs of the next-generation single-aisle aircraft. SVTT also develops computer modeling and simulation tools to study the noise and performance of multi-rotor urban air mobility vehicles.

Purpose

SVTT subsonic aircraft research enables revolutionary advancements in future aircraft performance to keep the nation ahead of global competitors.

Next-Generation Fixed-Wing Aircraft

SVTT works to advance the next-generation single-aisle aircraft through efficient airframes, reduced fuel consumption and noise, and propulsion-airframe integration.

Project research benefits U.S. industrial competitiveness in the subsonic transport aircraft market and will open new markets for U.S. regional jets and smaller size aircraft. SVTT research includes new, efficient airframe designs, the emerging area of electrified aircraft propulsion, and the complementary gas turbine engine research needed to develop new engines to power the new vehicles.

Urban Air Mobility

SVTT develops modeling and simulation tools to explore the noise and performance of multi-rotor urban air mobility (UAM) vehicles. Vertical lift vehicles have the unique ability to operate in confined areas, as evidenced by the emerging UAM industry within the broader advanced air mobility industry.

Additionally, advanced vertical lift capabilities support public good missions, such as disaster relief, emergency services, and medical transport.

Timeline and Impact

Although the SVTT project focuses on the long-term technology timeframe, it also contributes to both near-term and mid-term progress by demonstrating useful technology improvements along the way.

Advanced Air Vehicles Program
articles.reasoning: The article describes NASA's SVTT project developing advanced technologies and tools for next-generation subsonic aircraft and urban air mobility vehicles, which aim to reduce operating costs, fuel consumption, and noise. It also highlights benefits such as supporting public good missions like disaster relief and emergency services, indicating broad societal impact and significant technological advancement.
articles.personality_title: NASA develops new technologies for quieter, cheaper subsonic aircraft and urban air vehicles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA is working on a project called Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT). This project focuses on creating new technologies for different types of aircraft that fly slower than the speed of sound, including planes that take off and land vertically and traditional fixed-wing planes.

**What happened** – The SVTT project is designing better aircraft parts and tools to make future single-aisle planes more fuel-efficient, quieter, and cheaper to operate. It also develops computer models to study how small multi-rotor vehicles used in cities can perform and how much noise they make.

**Impact** – This work helps U.S. companies stay competitive in the aircraft market by improving designs and creating new electric and gas turbine engines. The research also supports urban air mobility vehicles, which can fly in tight spaces and help with important missions like disaster relief, emergency medical transport, and rescue operations.

**What's next step** – The project will keep testing and improving these technologies over the coming years. This includes both long-term plans and near-term demonstrations that show how the new designs and tools work in real life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s SVTT project is advancing quieter, more efficient aircraft and urban flying vehicles that could lower costs and support important public services.
articles.personality_title_fr: La NASA développe de nouvelles technologies pour des avions subsoniques et véhicules urbains plus silencieux et économiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA travaille sur un projet appelé Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT). Ce projet crée de nouvelles technologies pour différents types d’avions volant à des vitesses inférieures à celle du son, y compris des avions à décollage et atterrissage vertical et des avions à ailes fixes classiques.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le projet SVTT conçoit de meilleures pièces et outils pour rendre les avions à couloir unique plus économes en carburant, plus silencieux et moins coûteux à exploiter. Il développe aussi des modèles informatiques pour étudier les performances et le bruit des petits véhicules multi-rotors utilisés en ville.

**Impact** – Ce travail aide les entreprises américaines à rester compétitives en améliorant les conceptions et en créant de nouveaux moteurs électriques et à turbine à gaz. La recherche soutient aussi les véhicules de mobilité aérienne urbaine, capables de voler dans des espaces restreints et d’aider lors de missions importantes comme les secours en cas de catastrophe et les transports médicaux d’urgence.

**Étape suivante** – Le projet continuera à tester et améliorer ces technologies dans les années à venir, avec des démonstrations à court et long terme pour montrer leur efficacité.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le projet SVTT de la NASA fait progresser des avions et véhicules volants urbains plus silencieux et efficaces qui pourraient réduire les coûts et soutenir des services publics essentiels.
articles.personality_title_es: La NASA desarrolla nuevas tecnologías para aviones subsónicos y vehículos urbanos más silenciosos y económicos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA está trabajando en un proyecto llamado Subsonic Vehicle Technologies and Tools (SVTT). Este proyecto crea nuevas tecnologías para diferentes tipos de aviones que vuelan a velocidades menores que la del sonido, incluyendo aviones que despegan y aterrizan verticalmente y aviones tradicionales de ala fija.

**Qué pasó** – El proyecto SVTT diseña mejores partes y herramientas para hacer que los futuros aviones de un solo pasillo sean más eficientes en combustible, más silenciosos y más baratos de operar. También desarrolla modelos informáticos para estudiar el rendimiento y el ruido de vehículos urbanos de varios rotores.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo ayuda a las empresas de EE. UU. a mantenerse competitivas mejorando diseños y creando nuevos motores eléctricos y de turbina de gas. La investigación también apoya los vehículos de movilidad aérea urbana, que pueden volar en espacios reducidos y ayudar en misiones importantes como socorro en desastres y transporte médico de emergencia.

**Próximo paso** – El proyecto continuará probando y mejorando estas tecnologías en los próximos años, con demostraciones a corto y largo plazo para mostrar cómo funcionan en la práctica.

**Frase clave** – El proyecto SVTT de la NASA avanza en aviones y vehículos voladores urbanos más silenciosos y eficientes que podrían reducir costos y apoyar servicios públicos esenciales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of sleek, futuristic subsonic aircraft and multi-rotor urban air mobility vehicles gracefully soaring above a stylized cityscape, with smooth flowing lines symbolizing advanced aerodynamics and integration, set against a soft sky of natural blues and gentle earth tones to evoke innovation and progress.

articles.title: Startup is building the first data centre to use human brain cells
articles.summary: Cortical Labs is building two data centres that will house its neuron-filled chips. The technology is still in the very early stages of development
articles.content:
Cortical Labs is building two data centres that will house its neuron-filled chips. The technology is still in the very early stages of development

A small number of companies are working on biological computers Floriana/Getty Images

Data centres use huge amounts of energy and chips are in high demand – could brain cells be the answer? Australia-based start-up Cortical Labs has announced it is building two “biological” data centres in Melbourne and Singapore, stacked with the same neuron-filled chips that it has demonstrated can play Pong or Doom.

Cortical Labs is one of a few companies developing biological computers, consisting of neuronal cells wired up to microelectrode arrays that can stimulate and measure the response of cells when fed data. Earlier this month, the firm demonstrated that its flagship computer, the CL1, could learn to play the game Doom in a week.

Now, Cortical Labs has revealed two data centres that it plans to build. The first, in Melbourne, will contain around 120 CL1 units. The second, which is being built in collaboration with the National University of Singapore, will house 20 CL1s initially, but the company hopes it will eventually contain 1000 units in a larger data centre, after regulatory approval. Cortical Labs says this will allow it to expand its cloud-based brain-computing service.

Biological computers like the CL1 are being built and tested by research groups around the world, but they are often hard to build and not easy for others to use, says Michael Barros at the University of Essex, UK. “We spend a lot of money and sweat to build these [systems].”

“What [Cortical Labs] is doing is essentially allowing its biocomputer to be accessible at a large scale,” says Barros, who already uses cloud services from Cortical Labs as part of his research. “They’ll be the first ones to do that.”

Free newsletter Sign up to The Daily The latest on what’s new in science and why it matters each day. Sign up to newsletter

Though these systems can be trained for relatively simple tasks, like playing Doom, the exact way in which these neurons function and how best to train them to perform tasks like machine learning is still unclear, says Reinhold Scherer, also at the University of Essex. “Having access to this allows you to explore learning, training and programming,” he says. “You don’t program neurons like standard computers.”

Cortical Labs argues that its data centres will also require far less energy than typical computing systems, claiming that each CL1 needs around 30 watts, rather than the thousands of watts that a state-of-the-art conventional AI chip requires.

“When we scale up and have these as whole rooms, just like you have now with data servers, then there could be huge power savings,” says Paul Roach at Loughborough University, UK. There are other resources that biological data centres might need, such as nutrients to feed and keep alive the neuronal chips, but it should require far less cooling than conventional computing, he says. “The amount of energy that’s saved with [Cortical Labs’s] figures is fairly conservative.”

However, the technology is still at an early stage, says Tjeerd olde Scheper at Oxford Brookes University, UK, who has worked with a competing biological computing company, FinalSpark. “Is it going to work as people might think? No, we’re still in the early days of this development.”

It is hard to do a direct size comparison, as CL1 chips can’t do conventional calculations like a regular silicon-based AI chip can, but the proposed biological data centre will have hundreds of biological chips, compared with hundreds of thousands of graphics processing units (GPUs) seen in the largest AI data centres.

“I think it is a very long way from production-ready. It’s a very big step from a small network playing a computer game to an LLM, ” says Steve Furber at the University of Manchester, UK.

One of the remaining issues is that it is still unclear how to store the results of training these neurons in a form of memory, or how to run actual computational algorithms on them, rather than training them for specific uses like video games.

Another challenge is how to retrain the neurons once they have completed a particular task. “Whatever they are trained on is lost when the culture ends its life, so there needs to be a proper retraining,” says Scherer. “Then it’s not an optimal solution to keep a technology going if you need to retrain every 30 days.”
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a pioneering technological development where biological computers using human brain cells are being scaled up into data centres. This innovation promises significant energy savings compared to conventional AI chips, addressing a major environmental and technological challenge. The project is still in early stages but represents a meaningful breakthrough with broad potential impact on computing and energy consumption.
articles.personality_title: Startup builds data centres powered by human brain cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Data centres use a lot of electricity and need special computer chips to work. A new idea is to use human brain cells, called neurons, to make computers that use less energy.

**What happened** – An Australian company named Cortical Labs is creating two data centres, one in Melbourne and one in Singapore, filled with small devices called CL1 units. These units contain living neurons connected to tiny sensors. The company showed that their neuron computers can learn to play video games like Doom. The Singapore data centre will start with 20 units but could grow to 1,000 after approval.

**Impact** – This is the first time a company is making large data centres using living brain cells instead of regular computer chips. These biological computers use much less power—about 30 watts each—compared to thousands of watts for normal AI chips. This could save a lot of energy and reduce cooling needs in the future. However, the technology is still new and scientists are learning how to train and use these neuron computers effectively.

**What's next step** – Cortical Labs plans to expand its Singapore data centre and offer cloud services where more people can use these biological computers. Researchers will keep exploring how to store information in neurons and retrain them for different tasks. More testing and development are needed before this technology can be widely used.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Cortical Labs is building the first data centres using living brain cells to create energy-saving computers, marking an early but promising step in new computing technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une startup construit des centres de données alimentés par des cellules cérébrales humaines
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les centres de données consomment beaucoup d'électricité et ont besoin de puces spéciales pour fonctionner. Une nouvelle idée est d'utiliser des cellules cérébrales humaines, appelées neurones, pour fabriquer des ordinateurs qui consomment moins d'énergie.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une entreprise australienne nommée Cortical Labs crée deux centres de données, un à Melbourne et un à Singapour, remplis de petits appareils appelés unités CL1. Ces unités contiennent des neurones vivants connectés à de minuscules capteurs. La société a montré que ses ordinateurs neuronaux peuvent apprendre à jouer à des jeux vidéo comme Doom. Le centre de Singapour commencera avec 20 unités mais pourrait atteindre 1 000 après approbation.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois qu'une entreprise construit de grands centres de données utilisant des cellules cérébrales vivantes au lieu de puces informatiques classiques. Ces ordinateurs biologiques consomment beaucoup moins d'énergie — environ 30 watts chacun — contre des milliers de watts pour les puces d'IA normales. Cela pourrait économiser beaucoup d'énergie et réduire les besoins en refroidissement. Cependant, la technologie est encore nouvelle et les scientifiques apprennent comment entraîner et utiliser efficacement ces ordinateurs neuronaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Cortical Labs prévoit d'étendre son centre de Singapour et d'offrir des services cloud pour que plus de personnes puissent utiliser ces ordinateurs biologiques. Les chercheurs continueront à explorer comment stocker l'information dans les neurones et les réentraîner pour différentes tâches. Plus de tests et de développements sont nécessaires avant que cette technologie soit largement utilisée.

**En une phrase** – Cortical Labs construit les premiers centres de données utilisant des cellules cérébrales vivantes pour créer des ordinateurs économes en énergie, marquant une étape précoce mais prometteuse dans la nouvelle technologie informatique.
articles.personality_title_es: Startup construye centros de datos impulsados por células cerebrales humanas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los centros de datos usan mucha electricidad y necesitan chips especiales para funcionar. Una nueva idea es usar células cerebrales humanas, llamadas neuronas, para crear computadoras que consuman menos energía.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa australiana llamada Cortical Labs está creando dos centros de datos, uno en Melbourne y otro en Singapur, llenos de pequeños dispositivos llamados unidades CL1. Estas unidades contienen neuronas vivas conectadas a pequeños sensores. La empresa mostró que sus computadoras neuronales pueden aprender a jugar videojuegos como Doom. El centro en Singapur comenzará con 20 unidades pero podría crecer a 1,000 después de recibir aprobación.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que una empresa construye grandes centros de datos usando células cerebrales vivas en lugar de chips informáticos normales. Estas computadoras biológicas usan mucha menos energía, alrededor de 30 vatios cada una, comparado con miles de vatios para chips de IA normales. Esto podría ahorrar mucha energía y reducir la necesidad de enfriamiento. Sin embargo, la tecnología aún es nueva y los científicos están aprendiendo cómo entrenar y usar estas computadoras neuronales de manera efectiva.

**Próximo paso** – Cortical Labs planea expandir su centro en Singapur y ofrecer servicios en la nube para que más personas puedan usar estas computadoras biológicas. Los investigadores seguirán explorando cómo almacenar información en las neuronas y cómo reentrenarlas para diferentes tareas. Se necesitan más pruebas y desarrollo antes de que esta tecnología se use ampliamente.

**Una frase clave** – Cortical Labs está construyendo los primeros centros de datos usando células cerebrales vivas para crear computadoras que ahorran energía, marcando un paso temprano pero prometedor en la nueva tecnología informática.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a futuristic data centre room softly glowing with gentle amber light, filled with transparent, organic microchips resembling clusters of delicate human brain neurons interconnected by fine golden filaments, surrounded by subtle hints of nurturing elements like soft nutrient droplets and gentle electrical pulses symbolizing energy-efficient biological computing.
